questions.question: A minimum of how many satellite observations are required for a full 3D GNSS position fix?

answerOptions.text: 3 satellites
answerOptions.rationale: Three satellites only resolve a 2D position (latitude and longitude) and require height aiding.

answerOptions.text: 4 satellites
answerOptions.rationale: Four unknowns must be solved: latitude, longitude, height, and receiver clock error, requiring a minimum of four observations.

answerOptions.text: 6 satellites
answerOptions.rationale: Six or more satellites improve geometry and redundancy but are not the minimum required.

answerOptions.text: 2 satellites
answerOptions.rationale: Two satellites only define the intersection of two spheres, which is insufficient for positioning.

questions.hint: Think about the four unknowns that must be solved in every GNSS position fix.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of PPP over RTK for offshore survey positioning?

answerOptions.text: PPP provides centimetre-level accuracy without convergence time
answerOptions.rationale: PPP requires a convergence period of 20-30 minutes and typically achieves decimetre-level accuracy, not centimetre-level.

answerOptions.text: PPP provides global coverage without requiring a local base station
answerOptions.rationale: PPP uses a global network of reference stations and corrections delivered via satellite L-band, making it ideal for offshore use without local infrastructure.

answerOptions.text: PPP has faster update rates than RTK
answerOptions.rationale: Update rates are comparable; the advantage is coverage, not update rate.

answerOptions.text: PPP is immune to ionospheric scintillation
answerOptions.rationale: PPP is also affected by ionospheric scintillation, which can cause loss of L-band corrections and degraded positioning.

questions.hint: Consider what infrastructure is needed for each system and where offshore surveys operate.

questions.question: During a dynamic GNSS intersystem check, why is a box pattern covering all four cardinal headings required?

answerOptions.text: To test the GNSS receivers at different satellite geometries
answerOptions.rationale: Satellite geometry changes with time, not vessel heading. The box pattern serves a different purpose.

answerOptions.text: To expose heading-dependent offset errors such as antenna position offset errors
answerOptions.rationale: A wrong antenna offset produces position errors that reverse with heading changes. Sailing all four cardinal headings makes these biases visible as systematic patterns in the residuals.

answerOptions.text: To calibrate the vessel's gyrocompass
answerOptions.rationale: The gyrocompass is not calibrated during this check; it is a supporting sensor.

answerOptions.text: To verify the correction service works in all directions
answerOptions.rationale: Correction services are not directional; they work regardless of vessel heading.

questions.hint: Think about what happens to computed CRP position when the vessel turns 180 degrees if there is an antenna offset error.

questions.question: Without DVL aiding, approximately how far does a standard offshore INS drift in 4 minutes?

answerOptions.text: Approximately 1 metre
answerOptions.rationale: This is far too optimistic for unaided inertial navigation.

answerOptions.text: Approximately 12 metres
answerOptions.rationale: A standard offshore FOG-based INS drifts approximately 12 m in 4 minutes when unaided, and the drift rate accelerates over time.

answerOptions.text: Approximately 100 metres
answerOptions.rationale: This overstates the drift for 4 minutes, though drift does grow non-linearly over longer periods.

answerOptions.text: Approximately 0.1 metres
answerOptions.rationale: This would only be achievable with DVL aiding over a very short distance.

questions.hint: This figure illustrates why the DVL is the single most important aiding sensor for subsea INS operations.

questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between how an INS handles random errors versus systematic errors from aiding sensors?

answerOptions.text: The INS can correct both random and systematic errors through the Kalman filter
answerOptions.rationale: The Kalman filter can smooth random errors but has no mechanism to identify or correct systematic errors.

answerOptions.text: The INS smooths random errors effectively but cannot correct systematic errors
answerOptions.rationale: The INS improves precision by smoothing random noise, but it cannot identify where truth is. Systematic errors (constant offsets) pass through the filter unchanged.

answerOptions.text: The INS rejects both random and systematic errors from aiding sensors
answerOptions.rationale: The INS does not reject systematic errors; it absorbs them into its solution.

answerOptions.text: The INS only handles random errors; systematic errors are corrected by the DVL
answerOptions.rationale: The DVL provides velocity aiding but does not correct systematic errors in other aiding sensors like USBL.

questions.hint: Consider what the Kalman filter can and cannot do with data that is consistently offset from truth.

questions.question: When transferring USBL calibration results from a QINSy report into the USBL software, what critical step must be taken?

answerOptions.text: Multiply all values by the scale factor
answerOptions.rationale: The scale factor is applied separately; this is about the sign convention.

answerOptions.text: Invert the sign of every misalignment value
answerOptions.rationale: QINSy and USBL software use different sign conventions. Failure to invert signs doubles the misalignment error instead of correcting it.

answerOptions.text: Enter the values exactly as reported
answerOptions.rationale: This is correct for CASIUS reports, but NOT for QINSy reports where signs must be inverted.

answerOptions.text: Add 180 degrees to the heading misalignment only
answerOptions.rationale: The correction applies to all axes (heading, pitch, roll) and involves sign inversion, not adding 180 degrees.

questions.hint: CASIUS reports can be entered directly, but QINSy reports require a specific conversion.

questions.question: What is the achievable positional accuracy target for a modern USBL system?

answerOptions.text: Better than 0.01% of slant range
answerOptions.rationale: This is more typical of LBL systems in good conditions, not USBL.

answerOptions.text: Better than 0.3% of slant range plus surface positioning error at 95% confidence
answerOptions.rationale: Modern USBL systems should achieve accuracy better than 0.3% of slant range plus the absolute accuracy of the vessel surface positioning system at 2-sigma/95%.

answerOptions.text: Better than 1% of slant range
answerOptions.rationale: This is too pessimistic for modern systems; 0.3% is the target for well-calibrated USBL.

answerOptions.text: Better than 5 metres regardless of depth
answerOptions.rationale: USBL accuracy is depth-dependent, expressed as a percentage of slant range, not a fixed value.

questions.hint: The answer is expressed as a percentage of slant range plus a surface component.

questions.question: During an LBL array calibration, what is the maximum acceptable RMS of position residuals?

answerOptions.rationale: This is too loose; 0.50 m is the horizontal position standard deviation threshold for surface calibration.
Calibration range residuals (observed minus computed ranges) should be less than 0.10 m RMS for each transponder.
This is far too large for LBL calibration residuals and would indicate a significant problem.
While LBL can achieve centimetre precision in operations, calibration residuals of < 0.01 m are unrealistically tight.

questions.hint: This is the threshold for the difference between observed and computed ranges during calibration.

questions.question: Why is LBL more sensitive to sound velocity errors than USBL?

answerOptions.text: LBL uses higher acoustic frequencies that are more affected by SV changes
answerOptions.rationale: Frequency does not determine SV sensitivity; it is the acoustic path geometry that matters.

answerOptions.text: LBL acoustic paths are longer and more horizontal, crossing thermoclines at shallow angles
answerOptions.rationale: Horizontal paths spend more distance in the SV gradient zone. A 1 m/s SV error over a 500 m path produces approximately 0.33 m of range error.

answerOptions.text: LBL transponders do not have sound velocity sensors
answerOptions.rationale: SVPs are used for both LBL and USBL; the difference is in path geometry, not sensor availability.

answerOptions.text: USBL systems automatically compensate for sound velocity errors
answerOptions.rationale: Both systems need SVP input; USBL simply has shorter, more vertical paths that are less affected.

questions.hint: Consider the direction of acoustic paths in LBL (horizontal) versus USBL (primarily vertical).

questions.question: What does the HiPAP USBL spin test verify, and what is the acceptance criterion for 95% of position fixes?

answerOptions.text: It verifies heading accuracy; 95% of points within 0.1% of slant range
answerOptions.rationale: The spin test primarily verifies pitch/roll corrections and overall system accuracy, not just heading.

answerOptions.text: It verifies system accuracy after calibration; 95% of points within 0.3% of slant range plus surface positioning error
answerOptions.rationale: The spin test rotates the vessel 360 degrees to verify that USBL positions are consistent across all headings after calibration corrections are applied.

answerOptions.text: It verifies DVL calibration; 95% of points within 1% of distance travelled
answerOptions.rationale: The spin test is for USBL verification, not DVL calibration.

answerOptions.text: It verifies GNSS accuracy; 95% of points within 0.5 metres
answerOptions.rationale: The spin test verifies USBL acoustic positioning, not GNSS.

questions.hint: The vessel rotates through 360 degrees above a transponder during this test.

questions.question: What is the recommended minimum calibration depth for a HiPAP USBL system to produce results valid for all operating depths?

answerOptions.rationale: 50 m is too shallow; acoustic position errors would not be dominant over GNSS errors.

answerOptions.text: 200 m or deeper
answerOptions.rationale: A calibration at 200 m or deeper is valid for all operating depths. The optimum range is 400-1000 m. At shallower depths, the calibration is only valid for operating depth up to twice the calibration depth.

answerOptions.text: 1000 m minimum
answerOptions.rationale: 1000 m is within the optimum range but is not the minimum; 200 m is sufficient for universal validity.
At 100 m, the calibration is only valid for depths up to 200 m, not for all operating depths.

questions.hint: The calibration depth must ensure that acoustic errors dominate over surface positioning errors.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a GNSS Accurate Height Check, and what is the minimum data collection duration?

answerOptions.text: Verify horizontal accuracy; minimum 2 hours of data
answerOptions.rationale: This check is specifically for vertical (height) accuracy, not horizontal.

answerOptions.text: Verify GNSS-derived ellipsoidal heights against tide gauge data over one full tidal cycle (8-9 hours)
answerOptions.rationale: The check compares GNSS-derived water levels against independent 3rd-party tide gauge data over at least one full tidal cycle including one high and one low slack water.

answerOptions.text: Calibrate the GNSS antenna height; minimum 30 minutes
answerOptions.rationale: This is a verification, not a calibration, and 30 minutes is far too short to capture tidal variation.

answerOptions.text: Verify geoid model accuracy; minimum 24 hours
answerOptions.rationale: While geoid model quality does affect the result, 24 hours exceeds the minimum requirement of one full tidal cycle.

questions.hint: Think about what period is needed to capture the full range of tidal variation at a location.

questions.question: A CRPA (Controlled Reception Pattern Antenna) mitigates GNSS jamming by what mechanism?

answerOptions.text: Increasing signal transmission power to overpower the jammer
answerOptions.rationale: GNSS antennas are receive-only; they do not transmit.

answerOptions.text: Creating nulls in the antenna gain pattern in the direction of jammers using adaptive beam forming
answerOptions.rationale: CRPA uses adaptive beam forming to steer reception away from interference sources by creating directional nulls toward the jammer.

answerOptions.text: Encrypting the GNSS signal to prevent interception
answerOptions.rationale: Encryption is a feature of the satellite signal itself, not the antenna.

answerOptions.text: Switching to a backup frequency band not affected by jamming
answerOptions.rationale: CRPA works by spatial filtering (beam steering), not frequency hopping.

questions.hint: The antenna dynamically adjusts its reception pattern based on the direction of interference.

questions.question: In the context of tidal reduction, what does the form factor $F = (K1 + O1) / (M2 + S2)$ classify?

answerOptions.text: The accuracy of the tide gauge installation
answerOptions.rationale: The form factor classifies the tidal regime type, not gauge accuracy.

answerOptions.text: The tidal regime at a location (semi-diurnal, mixed, or diurnal)
answerOptions.rationale: $F < 0.25$ is semi-diurnal, $0.25 < F < 1.5$ is mixed (mainly semi-diurnal), $1.5 < F < 3.0$ is mixed (mainly diurnal), and $F > 3.0$ is diurnal.

answerOptions.text: The maximum tidal range at the survey location
answerOptions.rationale: The form factor classifies the type of tide, not its range.

answerOptions.text: The error margin in tidal predictions
answerOptions.rationale: The form factor describes the ratio of diurnal to semi-diurnal constituents, not prediction error.

questions.hint: The numerator contains diurnal constituents and the denominator contains semi-diurnal constituents.

questions.question: What is the key difference between Sonardyne SPRINT INS alignment and Exail (iXblue) PHINS/ROVINS alignment?

answerOptions.text: SPRINT requires a longer alignment time than PHINS
answerOptions.rationale: SPRINT has a simpler alignment process with no required dynamic manoeuvres.

answerOptions.text: SPRINT runs INS and AHRS concurrently without requiring alignment manoeuvres, while PHINS/ROVINS requires heading changes for fine alignment
answerOptions.rationale: SPRINT uses its AHRS (10-minute settle) to provide starting values to the INS, eliminating dynamic alignment manoeuvres. Exail systems need heading changes (figure-of-8 or stair pattern) to converge the Kalman filter.

answerOptions.text: PHINS systems do not require coarse alignment
answerOptions.rationale: PHINS requires a 5-minute coarse alignment phase where the system must remain stationary.

answerOptions.text: SPRINT cannot use DVL aiding during alignment
answerOptions.rationale: SPRINT uses all available aiding sensors; the difference is in the alignment methodology.

questions.hint: One system uses a concurrent AHRS to bootstrap the INS; the other uses dynamic manoeuvres to converge heading.

questions.question: A 10 ms timing error at a vessel speed of 4 knots (~2.1 m/s) produces what along-track position error?

answerOptions.text: Approximately 0.021 m
answerOptions.rationale: Position error = timing error x speed = 0.010 s x 2.1 m/s = 0.021 m.

answerOptions.text: Approximately 0.21 m
answerOptions.rationale: This would correspond to a 100 ms timing offset at 4 knots, not 10 ms.

answerOptions.text: Approximately 2.1 m
answerOptions.rationale: This overstates the error by a factor of 100.

answerOptions.text: Approximately 0.002 m
answerOptions.rationale: This understates the error by a factor of 10.

questions.hint: Multiply the timing error in seconds by the vessel speed in metres per second.

questions.question: What is the range error caused by an uncompensated 10 ms transponder latency with a sound velocity of 1500 m/s?

answerOptions.rationale: This is the one-way range for 0.5 ms, not the correct formula result.
Range error = latency (ms) x SV (m/s) / 2000 = 10 x 1500 / 2000 = 7.5 m. This systematic error directly affects USBL position.
This neglects the division by 2 for two-way travel time.
This would correspond to only 2 ms of latency, not 10 ms.

questions.hint: Use the formula: Range error (m) = latency (ms) $\times$ SV (m/s) / 2000

questions.question: When using GNSS tides for bathymetric reduction, what is the correct height relationship to derive height above chart datum?

answerOptions.text: Chart Datum Height = Ellipsoid Height + Geoid Undulation
answerOptions.rationale: The geoid undulation must be subtracted, not added, and the MSL-to-chart-datum separation must also be applied.

answerOptions.text: Height above CD = Ellipsoid Height - Geoid Undulation - MSL to Chart Datum Separation
answerOptions.rationale: Starting from the ellipsoidal height, subtract the geoid undulation to get MSL height, then subtract the MSL to chart datum separation to get height above chart datum.

answerOptions.text: Chart Datum Height = MSL Height multiplied by tidal range ratio
answerOptions.rationale: GNSS tides do not use tidal range ratios; they use the ellipsoid-geoid-datum separation chain.

answerOptions.text: Height above CD = Ellipsoid Height - Draft
answerOptions.rationale: Draft converts from antenna to waterline but does not account for the geoid and datum separations.

questions.hint: The conversion goes from ellipsoid to geoid to chart datum through two separation values.

questions.question: In USBL verification, what does a circular 'doughnut' pattern in the spin check indicate, and how can you distinguish an offset error from an angular error?

answerOptions.text: Sound velocity error; re-take the SVP to distinguish
answerOptions.rationale: SV errors typically manifest as scale issues and smile/frown shapes in transit lines, not doughnut patterns.

answerOptions.text: Residual pitch/roll/offset error; test at a different water depth to see if the doughnut size changes
answerOptions.rationale: An offset error produces a doughnut that does not change with depth, while an angular error produces a larger doughnut in deeper water and a smaller one in shallower water.

answerOptions.text: Heading sensor drift; compare with a second gyrocompass
answerOptions.rationale: Heading errors manifest as lateral displacement rather than a doughnut pattern.

answerOptions.text: GNSS multipath; relocate the antenna to fix it
answerOptions.rationale: GNSS multipath would affect surface positioning, not create a systematic subsea doughnut pattern.

questions.hint: The key diagnostic is to repeat the spin check at a different water depth and compare the doughnut size.

questions.question: What is the DVL-aided drift rate for a calibrated INS system, and what is the minimum verification interval during operations?

answerOptions.text: 0.5-1.0% of distance travelled; verify every 4 weeks
answerOptions.rationale: The drift rate is much better than 0.5%, and the interval is 8 weeks.

answerOptions.text: 0.02-0.06% of distance travelled; verify every 8 weeks
answerOptions.rationale: DVL-aided drift is typically 0.02-0.06% of distance travelled (0.2-0.6 m per km). Verification should be performed at minimum every 8 weeks while the system is in operation.

answerOptions.text: 0.1-0.5% of distance travelled; verify every 12 weeks
answerOptions.rationale: The drift rate is better than 0.1%, and the verification interval should be shorter than 12 weeks.

answerOptions.text: 1-2% of distance travelled; verify at project start only
answerOptions.rationale: This drift rate is far too pessimistic and verification must be repeated regularly during operations.

questions.hint: These figures represent typical DVL-aided performance and the recommended verification schedule.
